# Differentiate Instruction

## Connections to Community

Use these opportunities for informal science learning to provide local context and to extend and enhance unit concepts.

### At Home

PROPERTY RACE Ask students to have a competition with their family members. The student should race their family to see who can come up with the most properties for the largest number of items in their household within five minutes. For example, they could go around their house and write down a baseball, chair, hairbrush, and trashcan, and note what the shape, color, size, and weight is for the four objects. Use with Lesson 1. ELEMENT RANKING Have students work with family members to build an extended molecular structure out of household materials. For example, students could use pencils and balls of paper to build a model of table sugar. Students can present their molecules to the class. Use with Lesson 3.

## In the Community

PERIODIC TABLE Have students build a tactile periodic table by collecting examples of pure elements or substances with a particular element in it. Examples could include using aluminum foil for the Al square on the periodic table or using table salt (NaCl) for the Na square on the periodic table. Caution students against collecting hazardous materials for their periodic table. Have students invite younger students to the classroom to learn about and explore their periodic table. Use with Lesson 2.

### Culture

LANGUAGE AND SCIENCE The chemical symbols in the periodic table are the same in all languages, but the names of some elements may be different in different languages. Have students research the periodic table in one or more languages different from English and compare these to the periodic table in English. They should identify the similarities and differences between periodic tables. Students can also research how scientists and engineers who speak different languages communicate about their work. Students can create a poster to summarize their findings. Use with Lesson 2. Home Letters Use these letters to engage family members with unit concepts. # Differentiate Instruction

## Connections to Other Disciplines

Opportunities to connect to other content areas through Science and Engineering Practices or Crosscutting Concepts in this unit include Connection to Physical Science p. 15 Connection to Earth Science p. 29 Connection to Art p. 47 Connection to Biology p. 51 Connection to Social Studies pp. 53, 62 Connection to Life Science p. 62 Connection to Technology p. 62 Unit 1 The Structure of Matter 3H UNIT 1 The Structure of Matter Unit Project Overview and Planning Simple or Complex? 3D Learning Objective Students design and build a model of a complex carbohydrate and use it to show the relationship between simple molecules and extended structures. Students use the model to construct an explanation of how the structure of a carbohydrate relates to its function as an energy source in the body. Students research carbohydrates and their roles in the body. Students use the understanding of chemical and physical properties that they developed in Lesson 1. They will then synthesize the information from Lesson 3 to identify the differences between simple and complex carbohydrates in both their structures and their functions as energy sources for the body. Students will use modeling techniques from Lesson 3 to show the relationship between the structure of a simple molecule (simple carbohydrate) and an extended structure (complex carbohydrate).

# Getting Started

# Introducing the Project

Prepare students for their investigation by asking the Why It Matters questions in the Interactive Worktext and/or asking the following questions: What comes to mind when you hear the term carbohydrate (or"carb")? What do carbohydrates do in our bodies? What are some sources of carbohydrates?

# Student Deliverables

A student worksheet is available to help students in planning and completing the project. Students can be asked to turn in their worksheets, or they can be assessed on their models and presentations explaining the project. In addition to the worksheet, students build a model of a complex carbohydrate that shows the relationship between simple molecules and extended structures. The model could be a set of diagrams a physical model that shows the relationship between simple and complex carbohydrates an animation or other digital model ⚫a skit, dance, or other form of physical reenactment Students will use the model to construct an explanation of how the structure of each type of carbohydrate relates to its function as an energy source in the body. Students will choose a nutritional issue and will give a short presentation to explain the role of carbohydrates related to that issue. Presentations should include a description of the nutritional issue selected and the role of carbohydrates a claim for what types of carbohydrates are recommended a demonstration of how the model provides evidence to support the claim

# Differentiate Instruction

RTI/Extra Support Review Exploration 2 in Lesson 3, Analyzing the Structure of Matter. Focus on the section Models of Complex Molecules, and help students find the repeating unit in the PVC molecule shown. Point out that complex carbohydrates are also made up of repeating units (simple carbohydrates). Extension Challenge students to research artificial sweeteners and compare their structures and functions with natural carbohydrates such as sugar.

# Guiding Students on Project Planning

Once students understand the project goals and their deliverables, the next step is for students to conduct research on carbohydrates to learn about their composition and role in the body. Students will then model a complex carbohydrate and use the model to explain how different carbohydrates are broken down and used by the body. Have students choose an issue in nutrition related to how carbohydrates function in the body. Possible topics include the role of carbohydrates in diabetes, nutritional needs of elite athletes, the dietary needs of children and adults, or the difference in carbohydrate content of processed and unprocessed foods. To help students think about this project, consider asking • Are all carbohydrates the same? Students may know that there are simple and complex carbohydrates. Some students may know that fruits and grains contain different types of carbohydrates. What are the health effects of eating carbohydrates? What are some situations or conditions that affect how carbohydrates work in the body? Students may have heard of athletes "carbo-loading" before a big event, or of people with diabetes restricting their carbohydrate intake. Consider having students work in groups based on their interest in particular nutritional issues. For example, students who are interested in sports nutrition could work together, while students who are interested in comparing the dietary needs of children and adults could work together. To help students select a topic, consider brainstorming as a class to make a list of nutritional issues that are related to carbohydrate consumption. As students learn about how the structure of carbohydrates affects the way they function in the body, guide them to relate that information to their specific topic and to how different carbohydrates affect people. Be sure to review and approve project plans before students begin. The Unit Project Worksheet can be used for formal approval. # Guiding Students on Project Planning

Each student or group should research the chemical composition of carbohydrates. Guide students to books or Internet resources that provide simple but accurate information. Example: I learned that carbohydrates are made up of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen. There are simple carbohydrates and complex carbohydrates. Carbohydrates are pure substances because they are compounds made up of the same types of atoms in the same ratios throughout. I found information about carbohydrates on these websites [insert websites). 2 Students describe the relationship between simple and complex carbohydrates. Students will find that complex carbohydrates are made up of many simple carbohydrates (simple sugars) chemically bonded together. Students should realize that they can create multiple copies of a model of a simple carbohydrate and join them together to model a complex carbohydrate. Example: Complex carbohydrates are made up of many simple carbohydrates bonded together. The simple carbohydrates can be arranged in a chain or in branches. If I make a model of a simple carbohydrate, I can join together many copies of the model to model a complex carbohydrate. I found this information on [list website(s)]. 3 Students plan and create a model of a complex carbohydrate. Students should plan how they will build a model of a complex carbohydrate. Students' plans should include a model of a simple carbohydrate as a "base unit" that can be used to build a complex carbohydrate. The model will show a repeating pattern of simple molecules. Different materials should be made available for students to construct their models. Some models may be very simple, while others may be more complex. The repeating units should be clearly visible and identifiable in the finished model. Example: Our model will use paperclips. Each individual paperclip represents a simple carbohydrate, and a chain of paperclips represents a complex carbohydrate. 3K Unit 1 The Structure of Matter

# 4 Students research the role of carbohydrate foods in our bodies.

Students will describe the role of carbohydrates in providing energy to our bodies, and will give examples of food sources of simple and complex carbohydrates. Students should list websites used in their research. Example: Carbohydrates are a source of energy for our bodies. Some simple carbohydrates are glucose, fructose, sucrose, and lactose. Fructose is found in fruit and lactose is found in milk. Sucrose, or table sugar, comes from a plant. Starch is a complex carbohydrate. It is made up of many glucose molecules chemically bonded together. Starch is found in foods like bread and pasta. Simple carbohydrates are good for quick energy. This is because simple carbohydrates are digested very quickly. Complex carbohydrates take much longer to digest because there are many chemical bonds to be broken. The more complex the structure, the more slowly a carbohydrate is digested. I found this information on [list website(s)].

# 5 Students choose a nutritional issue to research.

Students can select a nutritional issue based on their own interests, or by choosing from a list of topics. Students should document their research and keep notes on how the structure of dietary carbohydrates affects the situation or condition selected. Example: I selected sports nutrition as a topic. Some athletes need immediate energy, such as a runner or swimmer before a short race. Simple carbohydrates can provide quick energy. For an endurance athlete, such as a marathon runner, complex carbohydrates digest more slowly and give energy over a longer time. I found this information on [list website(s)].

# 6 Students recommend a carbohydrate that would be beneficial for their selected issue.

Based on their research, students identify food sources that provide the appropriate type and amount of carbohydrates for a client with their nutritional issue. Students use this information to make a specific recommendation. Example: I recommend an orange for a sprinter or swimmer before a race. Fruit contains the simple carbohydrate fructose, which will digest quickly and give immediate energy. For a long-distance runner or cyclist, I recommend granola. Granola is made from oats, which contain complex carbohydrates. They digest slowly, so the energy is available over a longer period of time.

# 7 Students use their model to provide evidence for their recommendation and explain their reasoning.

Students use their model to demonstrate how simple and complex carbohydrates are used during digestion, and how this supports their recommendation. Students should explain that the individual pieces of the complex carbohydrate are simple carbohydrates. When these simple carbohydrates are not bonded together, they can be quickly digested by the body to produce energy. Students should use their model to demonstrate that it takes time to break all of the bonds. This is why it takes longer for the body to digest a complex carbohydrate. Students should include a brief summary of this on their worksheet. Example: The complex carbohydrate is made up of simple carbohydrates chemically bonded together. Simple carbohydrates are easy to digest because they are small and we get energy quickly after eating them. Eating simple carbohydrates raises a person's blood sugar levels quickly, which would not provide the long-term energy a marathon runner needs. Since there are many bonds in the complex carbohydrate, it takes a long time to break them all. This means a marathon runner will be supplied energy over a longer period of time.

# 7 Students use their model to provide evidence for their recommendation and explain their reasoning.

# Integrating the Three Dimensions of Learning

In this lesson, students discover what matter is and how it can be described through characteristic physical and chemical properties. They develop an understanding of how to use the properties of matter to explain behavior of substances (DCI PS1.A Structure and Properties of Matter). They explore mathematical models (SEP Developing and Using Models) that allow them to understand the relationship between weight and mass. They calculate the density of various objects after exploring how to calculate volume using two different methods (SEP Developing and Using Models, CCC Scale, Proportion, and Quantity).

## Preassessment

Have students complete the Unit Pretest online or see the Assessment Guide.

# Build on Prior Knowledge

Students should already know and be prepared to build on the following concepts: DCI The amount (weight) of matter is conserved when it changes form, even in transitions in which it seems to vanish. (Grade 5) DCI Measurements of a variety of properties can be used to identify materials. (Grade 5) CCC Graphs, charts, and images can be used to identify patterns in data. Give students several items of various shapes and weights, and a milk crate. Then challenge students to pack the crate in the most efficient way possible. Use this activity to get students thinking about why some objects can be squished to fit and others cannot, as well as why some items are heavier than others, or have different properties. # Content Background

Matter is anything that has mass and takes up space. Mass is a measure of how much matter makes up an object. It is important to note that while objects with mass are made of atoms, it is not enough to state that matter is anything made up of atoms and the subatomic particles that make up atoms. This is misleading because even subatomic particles themselves have mass and are made of matter. Emphasize to students that everything from the smallest particle to the most massive star is composed of matter. The amount of matter that makes up an object can be measured from its mass or calculated from its weight. Weight is a measure of the gravitational force acting on an object and therefore can change. For example, if the object were being weighed on a different planet that

An object can also be measured by its density, or how much mass it has per unit of volume. Density is an intensive property, meaning it does not depend on the quantity of the material. The volume of a regular-shaped object can generally be calculated using a mathematical formula. Physical properties such as density, color, or texture can be measured without changing the identity of an object or substance. Reacting with water, or other properties that cause an object to change its identity, is an example of a chemical property.

## ELL

Emphasize to students that mass and weight are often used interchangeably in everyday usage, but they mean different things in science. Explain to students that mass is always the same, but weight changes based on location. Ask students to think about an object on different planets and explain whether its mass and weight will be the same on both planets. # EXPLORATION 1 Defining Matter

## 3D Learning Objective

### Matter

All matter, including basketballs and water, shares two properties. Matter is anything that has mass and takes up space. Both living and nonliving things are matter. Your body, a tiny ant, and the rocks and soil in a garden are all matter. Some things that you cannot see are also matter. Air is matter. It takes up space inside a basketball. However, the actual flame that you see in a fire is a combination of light and heat. Neither light nor heat have mass or take up space. Because fire does not have mass or take up space, fire is not matter. 4. Which of the following would be considered matter? Choose all that apply. A. light from the sun B. milk in a carton. Mass depends only on the amount of matter in an object. It does not depend on the shape of object. The mass of an object does not necessarily depend on its size, either. # EXPLORATION 1 Defining Matter

## 3D Learning Objective

### Matter

When you pick up an object to judge how heavy it is, you are not actually measuring how much matter is in the object. What you are feeling is the object's weight. Weight is a measure of the gravitational force on an object. The gravitational force keeps objects on Earth from floating into space. Scientists measure weight using the unit newtons (N). Weight is also commonly measured in pounds (lb). The greater the mass of an object, the greater the gravitational force on the object. Therefore, the more mass an object has, the greater the object's weight is. For example, the two pieces of clay from the photo have the same weight on Earth because they have the same mass. However, weight also depends on gravity. The strength of the gravitational force affects the weight. Gravity is greater on Jupiter than on Earth, because Jupiter has more mass than Earth. Therefore, the clay pieces would weigh more on Jupiter. But their masses would be the same on Jupiter as they are on Earth. The only way to change an object's mass is to add or remove matter. # EXPLORATION 1 Defining Matter

## 3D Learning Objective

### Matter

When astronauts landed on the moon, they could carry very heavy pieces of equipment. They could also jump much farther than they could on Earth. They did all of this while wearing a spacesuit that weighed about 180 lb on Earth. Wearing the spacesuit on Earth would be like carrying around another person. Astronauts train for their missions to space, but they do not have superhuman strength. The astronauts' abilities on the moon are related to the mass of the moon, which is much smaller than the mass of Earth. On the moon, astronauts can carry objects that would be too heavy to carry on Earth. 9. Acceleration due to gravity on the moon is greater/less than acceleration due to gravity on Earth. The mass of an object on the moon is less than/greater than/the same as it is on Earth. Using the equation F ma to calculate weight, the mass of an object is multiplied/divided by acceleration due to gravity. So, the weight of an object on the moon is greater/less than the weight of the same object on Earth. FORMATIVE ASSESSMENT Ask: A nugget of gold and a nugget of iron pyrite both have a mass of 30 g. # 3D Learning Objective

## Volume

You may describe the size of an object by calling it big or small. You can also measure its volume. Volume is a measure of the amount of space an object takes up. It is the three-dimensional size of the object, which is why the units for volume are cubic centimeters (cm³) or cubic meters (m³). The volume of liquids and gases can also be measured in milliliters (mL) or liters (L). One milliliter equals one cubic centimeter. Volume can be measured in several different ways. The volume of a gas, such as air, is always the same as the volume of the container that the gas is in. The volume of a liquid, like water, is measured with tools such as graduated cylinders or beakers. The volume of a solid is measured in different ways depending on the shape of the object. Measure Volume with a Formula You can calculate the volume of regular-shaped objects using measurements. Regular-shaped objects include a rectangular box (prism), a spherical ball, or a cylindrical tube. To find the volume of a rectangular prism, you measure the length, width, and height of the prism. Then, you multiply those measurements using the formula: V=length x width x height 10 Unit 1 The Structure of Matter 10 Unit 1 The Structure of Matter Houghton Mifflin Harcourt Image Cre: OHM 11. Heughton Mifflin Harcou Measure Volume Using Displacement Many objects, such as a rock or a fork, have irregular shapes. Their length, height, or width depends on what part of the object you measure. You cannot use a simple formula to calculate the volume of these objects. Instead, you can measure the volume of liquid that is displaced by the object. This method, called displacement, works because two pieces of matter cannot be in the same space. When an object is placed in water, the water is moved out of the way, or displaced. # 3D Learning Objective

## Volume

The volume and mass of a given material can change. Pouring out half a glass of water decreases the volume and mass by half. However, the density of the water does not change. Density is the ratio of the mass of a substance to its volume. The more mass in a given volume, the greater the density of the object. For example, the air-filled balloon has a greater density than the helium-filled balloon. The air-filled balloon has more matter in the same amount of space. You can calculate the density (D) of an object by dividing its mass (m) by its volume (V): D\equiv\frac{m}{V} The units for density are the units for mass divided by the units for volume. For liquids, the units for density are grams per milliliter (g/mL). For solids, the units for density are grams per cubic centimeter (g/cm³) or kilograms per cubic meter (kg/m²). 500ml This rock has an irregular shape The volume of water in the beaker is 300 mL. Matter has many properties that you can observe and measure. Some properties, such as mass and volume, depend on the amount of a material. Other properties, such as density, are the same for any amount of a material. Liquid water in a glass has the same density as a drop of water on the side of the glass. Density is a characteristic property that depends on the material. It can be used to help you identify a material. Properties can also be divided into physical properties and chemical properties. Physical Properties Mass, volume, density, and state are physical properties. A physical property is a property that can be observed without changing the identity of the substance. You can describe some physical properties just by looking at an object, including shape, color, and size. You can describe the texture by feeling an object. Some physical properties are also characteristic to a material. For instance, pieces of pure copper can all have different masses, but they will all melt at the same temperature. 18. # Integrating the Three Dimensions of Learning

In this lesson, students develop an understanding of how physical and chemical properties can be used to identify substances (DCI PS1.A Structure and Properties of Matter). They gather data that enables them to calculate and use density values to identify specific elements. They explore mathematical analogies that allow them to model the scale of an atom (CCC Scale, Proportion, and Quantity). Students model the scale of an atom and explore how the periodic table is used as a model to organize the properties of elements (SEP Developing and Using Models).

# Content Background

Matter can be divided into pure substances and mixtures. Pure substances are either elements (made up of only one type of atom) or compounds (made up of only one type of molecule). The smallest particle of an element that retains its properties is an atom. Atoms are the building blocks of matter. There are 118 known elements; the first 94 occur naturally on Earth, while the remaining elements have been synthesized in the laboratory. These human-made elements have extremely short half-lives and generally do not have practical uses. Elements are arranged in the periodic table. In the 1860s, Dimitri Mendeleev, a Russian chemist, developed one of the first charts that organized the elements known at the time based on repeating properties that he observed.

Patterns and X-rays Henry Moseley built his own equipment to prove that every element has its own x-ray frequency pattern. When atoms gain a lot of energy, they can release x-rays. Henry Moseley shot high-energy particles at samples of elements to energize the samples. He discovered that each element would then emit an x-ray pattern specific to that element. Moseley found that these x-rays followed a pattern. The x-ray pattern that each element emitted corresponded to a whole number. These whole numbers are now known as the atomic numbers of elements. In general, these atomic numbers increased as the atomic masses increased, but there were some variations. When the periodic table was arranged using atomic numbers instead of atomic mass, some inconsistencies of earlier periodic tables disappeared. # Integrating the Three Dimensions of Learning

In this lesson, students examine models of molecules and compare composition and structure in order to explain the differences among substances (DCI PS1.A Structure and Properties of Matter). Students develop and use models to describe molecules and analyze the arrangements of extended structures (SEP Developing and Using Models). They also examine structures that exist on an extremely small scale (CCC Scale, Proportion, and Quantity).

## Preassessment

Have students complete the unit pretest online or see the Assessment Guide.

## Build on Prior Knowledge

Students should already know and be prepared to build on the following concepts: Matter is anything that has mass and volume. All matter is composed of atoms, and there are different kinds of atoms. Different kinds of atoms are arranged in the Periodic Table of Elements. DCI Each pure substance has characteristic physical and chemical properties (for any bulk quantity under given conditions) that can be used to identify it. Have students identify examples of matter in their school. They should discuss the differences in properties of those example substances and should explain how different substances have different properties. Professional Development Go online to view Professional Development videos with strategies to integrate CCCs and SEPs, including the ones used in this lesson.

## Content Background

The atomic structure of matter exists on a scale that is too small to see with the unaided eye. Make sure students understand that atomic structure is too small to see with even strong microscopes. It is this structure and the types of atoms that determine the observable properties of matter. It is also the reason that models are developed to study the structure of matter. Remind students that models have limitations, which is why scientists use different types of models to study matter. For example, it can be difficult to comprehend characteristics such as the size and complete shape of a compound when only a 2D model is used to represent atoms connected together. ## 3D Learning Objective

Students use models to analyze the arrangement of atoms in molecules. They explore how some substances are made from different types of atoms that combine with one another in specific ratios. Students start to recognize that they can use models to study systems that are too large or too small, such as the structure of matter. DCI PS1.A Structure and Properties of Matter Misconception Alert Students observe models to see that some substances are made from different types of atoms, which combine with one another in various ways. in the 18th century and early 19th century, French chemist joseph Proust worked and taught in France and Spain. He is best known for his work on what is called the law of definite proportions. This law states that a pure chemical compound always contains the same elements in exactly the same proportions by mass. Although many scientists assumed this to be true, it was Proust who first gathered evidence to support it Suppose you have a sample of sodium chloride, or table salt. You determine that the sample consists of 39% by mass of the element sodium and 61% by mass of the element chlorine, a ratio of 39% sodium to 61% chlorine. This proportion is true not only for this sample of sodium chloride, but oll samples of sodium chloride. No matter where or how this pure substance is found, this proportion holds true. # Support for Unit Connections

This section provides student choice in making cross-disciplinary connections to a variety of science and nonscience disciplines. Students can select one or more of the activities to complete on a separate sheet of paper.

## Technology Connection

Silicon is the second most abundant element in Earth's crust and is found in minerals, for example in quartz sand. Its atomic structure makes it a good semiconductor, which makes it easier to control electric signals. It is used to make computer chips, transistors, electric circuits, and switching devices. Silicon can be processed into very thin chips that can withstand high temperatures within electrical devices. Students' presentations should show they researched silicon and can give examples of how it is used in electronic devices and why it is used based on its properties.

## Life Science Connection

Proteins are complex molecules made of one or more chains of amino acids. Proteins carry out most of the cellular processes in living things. They function as enzymes and hormones, provide transport for materials and cellular structure and support, and aid in mobility. A familiar example of a protein is hemoglobin, which transports oxygen in the blood from the lungs to all cells in the body. Students should choose a protein to research and create a poster that includes a model of the protein and its function.

Answers should include that gel electrophoresis is used to separate large molecules on the basis of their size and charge. Molecules flow through the gel, which contains pores of various sizes. # Why It Matters

This section provides questions for students to make meaningful connections between the content and real-world issues and topics that might be relevant to their community. These questions can be used with the Unit Project, as discussion prompts throughout the unit, or as a student journaling activity. As students read through the questions, encourage them to brainstorm with other students if they have trouble thinking of answers. Guide students to remember that matter is in all different states around us and changes state often, which then affects our lives. Ask: What types of matter have you come in contact with today? Can you recall a time when you witnessed matter in two or more different states recently, for example a glass of water with ice cubes?

## Answers

These questions are designed to guide students to recognize the different states of matter and how often changes of state happen around them. Answering the questions enables students to realize that when matter changes state, it affects us in many ways. This activity can also be incorporated into the Unit Project in which students research the disappearance of Arctic ice. These questions can encourage students to connect changes of state to climate and life on Earth.
